Ladakh.Planning Your Ladakh trip: Essential Inf: The secret to making your trip to Leh Ladakh unforgettable is to plan well in advance. Due to its high elevation, extreme weather, and distinctive culture, the area is unlike your typical vacation destination and requires a bit more planning. Here's everything you need to know before you travel, whether you're considering Leh Ladakh trip packages or creating your own plan. Best Time to Visit Ladakh Your experience in Ladakh will be greatly influenced by the time of your vacation. Summer (May to September): The busiest travel season is during the summer. The skies are clear, the roads from Manali and Srinagar are still open, and the temperature is comfortable between 10°C to 22°C. This is the best time to see mountain passes, lakes, and monasteries. Around this time of year, the majority of Ladakh Tour Packages are created. Winter (November to March): Experienced travellers should use this time. Even though it can get as cold as -25°C, the frozen scenery, regional celebrations, and experiences like the Chadar Trek are once in a lifetime. Extreme weather causes some Leh Ladakh Family Tour Packages to forego this season, but thrill-seekers will remember it. Autumn (October): A photographer's paradise, it has fewer tourists, golden hues throughout the valleys, and crisp air. An excellent time if you like more sedate travel and less expensive Ladakh travel. How to Reach Ladakh The trip to Ladakh is just as unforgettable as the final destination. By Air: Flying into Kushok Bakula Rimpochee Airport in Leh, which has good connections to Delhi, Mumbai, and Srinagar, is the simplest and fastest choice. Flights save time when selecting Leh Ladakh car tour packages, but they also allow you to explore by road after you are there. By Road: The road trip is the high point for many people. Srinagar-Leh Highway (434 km): The Srinagar-Leh Highway (434 km) is a preferable route for acclimatisation because of its milder elevation. It is less difficult and more picturesque as it passes through Zoji La and Drass Valley. Manali-Leh Highway (473 km): The 473-kilometre Manali-Leh Highway is well-known among bikers and adventure seekers for its breathtaking views of mountain passes, rivers, and glaciers. This trip is included in the majority of Leh Ladakh group tour packages. By Bus or Rental Bike: Leh is connected to Srinagar and Manali by inexpensive buses. The majority of Ladakh travel packages now include the option to rent automobiles or bikes for a more daring experience, allowing you to explore at your own speed. Acclimatisation & Health Tips Getting used to the thin air is essential because Leh is 3,500 meters above sea level. Diet and Hydration: Consume four litres of water or more each day. At first, avoid smoking and drinking, and limit your intake to light meals. Rest First: Before continuing, stay in Leh for at least 36 to 48 hours. Don't do anything physically demanding on your first day. Medication: Ask your doctor about Diamox and other preventive medications. If trekking or high-altitude camping are included in your Ladakh package, bring portable oxygen cylinders. Recognise the Signs: Acute mountain sickness may manifest as nausea, headache, or lightheadedness. If symptoms get worse, get down right away. Your trip to Leh Ladakh is safer and more pleasurable when you are well acclimated. Permits and Regulations Because of Ladakh's delicate borders, permissions are needed in some places. Inner Line Permit (ILP): Indian nationals travelling to Nubra Valley, Pangong Tso, and Tso Moriri must have an Inner Line Permit (ILP). You can apply for these in person at the Leh DC office or online. A daily fee, an environment fee, and optional Red Cross donations are included in the costs. Observe Local Regulations: Permits may not remove restrictions in some areas. Sustainable tourism is ensured by honouring regional traditions and the delicate ecosystem. Protected Area Permit (PAP): Foreign nationals must have a Protected Area Permit (PAP), which is granted by authorised organisations. These typically require a minimum of two travellers and are valid for seven days. Multiple copies: Keep several copies with you because they will be requested at checkpoints across Ladakh.
Ladakh.Curated Ladakh Tour Packages by Thomas C: Thomas Cook's Leh Ladakh Tour Packages are ideal if you've been dreaming of snow-capped mountains, azure lakes, and historic monasteries tucked away high in the Himalayas. These are travels rather than vacations, where each road leads to a tale just waiting to be discovered, and each sunrise seems like a painting. With carefully prepared itineraries, these Ladakh Tour Packages give you the appropriate blend of adventure, culture, and comfort, no matter what kind of traveller you are. Introduction to Packages The flexibility offered by Thomas Cook when arranging a Leh Ladakh package is among its best features. There is something for every mood, whether you are looking for a short 4-night getaway to Leh or a longer excursion that takes you deep into Nubra Valley and Pangong Lake. You can choose between a mid-range vacation, a luxury retreat with boutique hotels and carefully chosen experiences, or a journey that is more affordable with these Ladakh trip packages, which come in three different tiers: Standard, Value, and Premium. Package Categories (Honeymoon, Family, Solo & Others) Thomas Cook's Ladakh trip packages include everything you need for a smooth trip, whether you're organising a romantic getaway, a family vacation, or an exciting adventure. Honeymoon packages: The honeymoon-focused Leh Ladakh trip packages are ideal if you're searching for romance surrounded by mountains and starry skies. Imagine camping near Pangong Lake, eating a candlelit meal under the stars, or wandering hand in hand through Nubra Valley’s sand dunes. Private transports, upscale accommodations, and customised extras are typically included in these packages. This kind of Ladakh trip packages promises lifelong memories. Solo and Group tours: Do you want to avoid feeling lonely while travelling alone? The group travel packages for guided Leh Ladakh are ideal. Along with a well-planned schedule that handles everything, from permits to meals, you also have the company of other travellers and the assistance of a tour manager. Thomas Cook also provides exhilarating bike trips for adventurers and bikers, perfect for solo or group rides. Additionally, you can customise your Ladakh trip cost if you're more of a flexible person. Family Packages Thomas Cook has created carefully considered Leh Ladakh Family Tour Packages for individuals who are travelling with parents or kids. These itineraries are leisurely, including cosy lodgings, secure transportation, and family-friendly activities. These packages emphasise family-bonding activities, such as visiting monasteries like Thiksey and taking camel rides in Nubra. The Ladakh trip cost is kept affordable, and there are possibilities for travelling with multiple generations.
Ladakh.Top Attractions & Experiences in Ladakh: Ladakh evokes images of glistening lakes, bare mountains, and monasteries looming over the landscape. A trip to Leh Ladakh, however, is much more than just the landscape; it's an adventure, a cultural exploration, and a lifelong memory. Here are some experiences you simply must have when planning your own vacation to Ladakh or perusing several Leh Ladakh travel packages. Nubra Valley: Imagine traversing a frigid desert on a double-humped camel. Nubra is for you. Almost every Leh Ladakh package includes the Hunder dunes, monasteries, and apricot orchards as highlights. This stop is a mainstay of Leh Ladakh family tour packages since families particularly like it. Pangong Lake: Nothing compares to seeing Pangong in real, even though you've undoubtedly seen it in movies. Every moment is stunning as the light transforms the colours of this high-altitude lake. A night stay beside Pangong is included in the majority of Ladakh trip packages; it's weird to wake up here. Tso Moriri: Ladakh's secret gem is Tso Moriri, which is much quieter than Pangong. Because it draws rare species, bird lovers will appreciate this location. For a more tranquil experience, try to incorporate it in your Ladakh tour package, even though not all of them do. Khardung La Pass: Khardung La is one of the world's highest motorable passes at more than 18,000 feet. It seems like a success to cross it. This path will give you chills at every bend if you choose an adventurous Leh Ladakh group tour packages or even a Leh Ladakh car tour packages. Monasteries: Monasteries such as Hemis, Thiksey, and Diskit are scattered throughout Ladakh. Each monastery connects you to the local culture, offers peace, and tells a tale. These cultural stops enhance your tour, regardless of the expense of your Ladakh trip. Magnetic Hill: Watch your car drive uphill by itself after you park it in neutral. Numerous Leh Ladakh car travel packages incorporate this optical illusion on the Srinagar-Leh highway. It's entertaining, eccentric, and eye-opening. Sangam: The confluence of the Indus and Zanskar rivers is truly beautiful. Despite meeting, the two rivers maintain their unique colours. Many Ladakh travel packages include this vista, which is frequently combined with river rafting for adventurers. Stargazing: Ladakh is a stargazer's heaven because of its pristine skies. There is little doubt that the night sky in Ladakh will leave you speechless, regardless of whether you are travelling in luxury or on a more affordable trip package.
Ladakh.Things to Do in Ladakh Beyond Sightseein: It's easy to get sucked into the famed lakes, monasteries, and high-altitude routes when organising a trip to Leh Ladakh. However, Ladakh is so much more than just a place to admire the scenery. To make your journey to Ladakh genuinely unforgettable, the area welcomes you to immerse yourself in adventure, culture, and cuisine. Here are some distinctive experiences that enhance your trip, regardless of whether you are booking Leh Ladakh Tour Packages, Leh Ladakh car tour Packages, or even looking into possibilities like Leh Ladakh group tour packages. River Rafting in Zanskar and Indus Imagine speeding down the freezing Zanskar Gorge, sometimes referred to as the "Grand Canyon of Asia." Here, river rafting is both exciting and picturesque—a combination you won't soon forget. The section of the Indus River close to Nimo has milder rapids if you'd rather have something lighter. Rafting alternatives are now included in a lot of Leh Ladakh car tour packages, allowing you to enjoy adventure without worrying about practicalities. Trekking through Hidden Trails Ladakh trekking is like entering another universe. The Sham Valley Trek, often known as the "Baby Trek," is ideal for beginners and families with young children because it winds past historic towns and apricot orchards. For experienced hikers, the Rumtse to Tso Moriri walk or the Chadar Trek across the frozen Zanskar River will push you to the limit while rewarding you with unparalleled landscapes. You can have an experience that goes much beyond the standard Leh Ladakh Tour Packages by including trekking in your travel packages. Biking Across High Passes Ladakh is the best place to go motorcycling if you want an adrenaline thrill. Pure freedom on wheels can be experienced whether riding a motorcycle over the Manali–Leh route or cycling down Khardung La, one of the highest motorable passes in the world. Many Leh Ladakh package trips now include biking as a top attraction, particularly for adventurers taking part in Leh Ladakh group travel packages. Immersion in Local Culture Making connections with the locals is one of the most fulfilling aspects of visiting Leh Ladakh. You may learn about weaving, eat with Ladakhi families, and even see traditional games like polo or archery while you stay in a homestay. You can see centuries-old customs and vibrant mask dances if you plan your trip around the Hemis Festival or Losar. Any Ladakh package is enhanced and given greater significance by these experiences. Observing the Stars Through a Clear Himalayan Sky Ladakh has some of the clearest skies in the world. It seems fantastic to just lie beneath a starry sky after an adventurous day. Regardless of the Ladakh trip cost, stargazing is free and gives you treasured memories, whether you're travelling with a family or alone. Savouring Regional Ladakhi Cuisine In Ladakh, food is more than just sustenance; it's ingrained in the culture. Every meal seems cosy, from filling meals like chutagi (dumpling soup) and skyu (a pasta-like soup) to butter tea and khambir bread. Don't overlook the region's specialities, such as sea buckthorn juice and apricot jams. You will feel as though you have experienced the essence of Ladakh if you include culinary trails in your travel packages.
Ladakh.Essential Tips for a Smooth Ladakh trip: It's exciting to plan your Leh Ladakh Tour Packages, but due to the high elevation and isolated landscape, careful planning is necessary. These suggestions will make your trip easy and stress-free, whether you're using Leh Ladakh Tour Packages, Leh Ladakh family tour packages, or exciting Leh Ladakh group tour packages. Packing Checklist: You may be surprised by Ladakh's weather, which features chilling winds at night and warm sun throughout the day. Bring layers: light T-shirts at lower elevations, fleece jackets, thermals, and a thick windproof coat. Essentials include headgear, woollen gloves, a good pair of walking shoes, and sunglasses that block UV rays. Remember to pack a first aid kit, lip balm, and sunscreen with a high SPF. Consult your physician about prophylactic medications if you're concerned about altitude sickness. ATMs and banking: In Ladakh, money is everything. Leh has many ATMs, but they frequently run out of power. As soon as you get there, take out what you need, and make sure you have enough for food, petrol, and minor purchases. Since digital payments are uncommon outside of Leh, budget additional money for your journey to Ladakh. Connectivity: You will need a postpaid SIM card because prepaid SIM cards from outside Ladakh do not function. Jio and Airtel are good in Leh; however, BSNL is only available in isolated places. Inform your loved ones in advance of any impending blackouts in the vicinity of Pangong, Nubra, or Tso Moriri. This is covered in many Ladakh travel packages, so you won't be surprised. Contacts for Emergencies: Keep the following numbers handy: Ambulance 102, Police 100, and Tourist Helpline 1363. Travellers in need are frequently assisted by Army and BRO installations in isolated locations. Responsible Travel: Ladakh is vulnerable because of its lack of water and persistent plastic trash. Bring a reusable bottle, steer clear of lengthy showers, and help out the community by booking homestays or purchasing souvenirs directly from the locals. In monasteries, observe customs and wear modest clothing at all times. Selecting environmentally friendly Ladakh holiday packages guarantees that your journey will benefit the area as well. These minor adjustments have a big impact on transforming your Leh Ladakh package from a simple vacation into a smooth, unforgettable experience.
Ladakh.Why Book Your Ladakh Tour with Thomas Co: Thomas Cook is the answer to your desire to enjoy the adventure without bothering about the planning, while you're dreaming of the ideal trip to Leh Ladakh. Every aspect of their Leh Ladakh Tour Packages is seamless and stress-free because they are created with the needs of the traveller in mind. Some options fit your style, whether you're looking for Leh Ladakh car tour packages to share the excitement with friends or family vacation packages to spend quality time together. Customised Ladakh travel packages and Leh Ladakh automobile tour packages are options if you'd rather have more freedom to take your time admiring the scenery. The best part is that you don't have to worry about how much your Ladakh trip cost will be because there are special offers and discounts to increase the value of your vacation. Every one of Thomas Cook's expertly planned Leh Ladakh Tour Packages turns into a memory that will last a lifetime, not just a vacation.

table_of_content: Best Places to Visit in Kyoto
Kyoto.Introduction: Kyoto, Japan’s heart of culture, tradition, and timeless beauty, is all set to welcome you! The city, with its ancient temples, peaceful gardens, and rich heritage, is a destination that will fill your soul and leave you mesmerised. With its cherry blossoms in spring and the fiery foliage that covers the entire city in autumn, Kyoto truly leaves visitors awestruck with its charm and the various historical treasures it houses. We, at Thomas Cook, offer thoughtfully curated Kyoto tour packages starting from just ₹1,99,900*. We provide you with a hassle-free way to explore the city, featuring spiritual getaways, cultural adventures, and even romantic escapes on your itinerary. In every package on offer, we ensure that you get to experience the best Kyoto tourist attractions, enjoy delicious local food, and immerse yourself in everything that is authentically aligned with Kyoto’s traditions. Here is everything you need to know about the city, the tour packages on offer, what to eat, where to go, and the like! Get set to discover the magic of Kyoto like never before.
Kyoto.Popular Kyoto Tour Packages: Here is a look at our top-selling Kyoto holiday packages, which are specifically designed to provide you with an unforgettable experience in Japan’s cultural heartland. Doesn’t matter if you are planning a short city escape or a multi-city adventure, our Tokyo packages work brilliantly for all kinds of travellers, including families, couples, and backpackers. Every package includes essentials like accommodation, local transportation, guided sightseeing, traditional meals at select places and even entry to major Tokyo attractions. Think of the historical streets of Gion and the tranquil bamboo groves of Arashiyama, and you will understand the kind of life-changing experience you might have here. Tour Packages NameDurationPriceGrand Bargain Japan Cherry Blossom (Kobe - Tokyo)7 Nights 8 DaysRs. ₹283 000Grand Bargain Japan Cherry Blossom (Tokyo - Tokyo)7 Nights 8 DaysRs. ₹283 000Japan Cherry Blossom (Tokyo-Kobe) - Pure Veg8 Nights 9 DaysRs. ₹330 500Japan Cherry Blossom (Tokyo - Kobe)8 Nights 9 DaysRs. ₹330 500Japan Cherry Blossom (Kobe - Tokyo)8 Nights 9 DaysRs. ₹330 500
Kyoto.Best Time to Visit Kyoto: One of the best things about Kyoto is that the city is an all-season destination. However, it is essential to note that your travel experience will vary depending on when you choose to visit the city. Kyoto undergoes a remarkable transformation with each passing season. Think cherry blossoms in spring, festivals in summer, dense, vivid foliage in autumn, and snow-covered peace in winter. When you understand how each season affects the city, you will be better able to pick the best time to visit Kyoto based on your interests. Spring (March to May) Spring is, without a doubt, the most magical time in Kyoto. You can easily spot cherry blossoms or sakura blooms across the city from parks and temple gardens to along riverbanks as well. If you are fascinated by flowers, then spots like Maruyama Park, Philosopher’s Path and Heian Shrine might just be ideal for you. The spring temperatures range from 10°C to 20°C, which makes it ideal for walking tours and temple visits. Do remember, though, that spring is the peak season, so it is important to book your Kyoto tour packages well in advance. Summer (June to August) Summer can be quite hot and humid with temperatures staying between 25°C and 35°C. However, summers are also about festivals and cultural activities in Kyoto. It is in July that you will get to experience Gion Matsuri, one of Japan’s largest and most colourful festivals. It is all about massive floats, street food, and traditional performances. If you are looking for a respite from the heat, though, then evenings by the Kamogawa River are pleasant, especially in June when the hydrangeas are in bloom. Autumn (October to November) Many travellers swear by visiting Kyoto in autumn, calling it the best season to visit. Imagine the city glowing with red, orange, and golden hues as the leaves change colour. To take in the true beauty of these colours, spots like Tofuku-ji, Eikan-do, and Arashiyama are popular among travellers. The air is crisp, and the temperature is pleasant, ranging from 10°C to 18°C, making it perfect for hiking and sightseeing. Winter (December to February) Winter just might be the most peaceful season to visit Kyoto during. Though cold, there is less crowd and the occasional snowfall. Temples like Kinkaku-ji under a layer of snow look picture perfect, while winter festivals, hot springs, and seasonal cuisine make it a worthwhile time to visit.
Kyoto.How to Reach Kyoto?: Another advantage of visiting Kyoto is that it is one of Japan’s most accessible cities. This is mainly due to the country’s efficient and world-renowned transportation network. Even though Kyoto doesn’t have its own international airport, it is connected to cities like Osaka and Tokyo through air, rail, and road. Here’s how you can reach the city, whether you're coming from overseas or another part of Japan By Air Yes, Kyoto does not have an airport, but it is well-connected to two domestic airports nearby: Kansai International Airport (KIX): The airport is located in Osaka and is about 75–90 minutes from Kyoto by train or airport limousine bus. JR Haruka Express also offers a direct and comfortable ride to Kyoto Station from and to KIX. Osaka Itami Airport (ITM): ITM primarily serves domestic flights and is situated around 50 km from Kyoto. There are direct limousine buses connecting Itami to Kyoto, and they take approximately 60 minutes to complete the trip. By Train One of the best ways to reach Kyoto is to travel by Japan’s high-speed Shinkansen, or bullet train. From Tokyo, you can take the Tokaido Shinkansen on either the Nozomi, Hikari, or Kodama lines. The fastest it will take you to Kyoto is 2 hours and 20 minutes. From Osaka, Kyoto is just 15 minutes away by the Shinkansen. If you take a regular JR Special Rapid service, it might take around 30 minutes. From Nagoya, you can reach Kyoto in 35–40 minutes by Shinkansen. Once you are at Kyoto Station, you can use local buses, taxis, and subways to make city navigation easy. By Road Kyoto is also accessible through long-distance buses and car rental options. From Tokyo, you can opt for overnight highway buses. These are budget-friendly and usually take around 8–9 hours to reach the city. From Osaka, many intercity buses run to Kyoto, taking about 90 minutes to reach the city. If you are looking to Kyoto, though not an impossibility, the narrow streets and limited parking do make public transportation the best option for most travellers.
Kyoto.Best Places to Visit in Kyoto: Kyoto is a truly timeless city that is famous for its temples, gardens, shrines, etc. With over 1,600 Buddhist temples and 400 Shinto shrines, most of which are UNESCO World Heritage Sites, the city truly has a deep cultural tie. Here is a look at some of the best places to see in Kyoto to help you understand the true essence of the city. Iconic Kyoto Tourist Attractions Fushimi Inari Taisha: One of the most photographed sites in Kyoto, the place is popular for its seemingly endless path of 10,000 vermilion torii gates. The shrine is located at the base of Mount Inari and is dedicated to the god of rice and commerce. Kinkaku-ji or the Golden Pavilion: The Golden Pavilion is a Zen Buddhist temple and is a treat to anyone’s eyes. With its shimmering gold leaf and perfect reflection in the surrounding pond, it is one of the most visited Kyoto tourist places. Those who are interested in photography will find this spot a true delight. Kiyomizu-dera Temple: The temple is a UNESCO-listed site and offers panoramic views of Kyoto from its famous wooden terrace. You need to check the temple out during cherry blossom season and autumn. Remember to try your luck at the Otowa Waterfall or explore the historic Sannenzaka and Ninenzaka streets nearby. Arashiyama Bamboo Grove: Think of walking through towering green bamboo stalks and experiencing a surreal, calming aura. Located near the scenic Togetsukyo Bridge, the grove also has a monkey park, riverboat rides, and peaceful temples for you to explore. Temples, Shrines, and Spiritual Sites Ryoan-ji TempleThe temple is home to Japan’s most famous rock garden and is known to promote meditation and introspection. The temple is minimally designed, making it both spiritual and symbolic. Yasaka ShrineThe shrine is located between Gion and Higashiyama, and it is the spiritual centre of the Gion Matsuri, which is held every July. You need to have a look at the shrine at night as it gets illuminated beautifully. Sanjusangen-do Temple The temple is known for housing 1,001 statues of Kannon, the goddess of mercy. It’s undoubtedly one of the most unique things to see in Kyoto and is a great place to visit for art lovers and history buffs. Tofuku-ji Temple Popular during autumn, this temple is famous for its dramatic display of red maple leaves and picturesque bridges. Cultural and Historical Experiences Gion DistrictKyoto’s geisha district takes you back in time with its wooden teahouses, traditional eateries, and occasional geisha sightings. This is one of the most culturally rich places to visit in Kyoto. Philosopher’s PathThe Philosopher’s Path is a tranquil stone path that follows a cherry tree-lined canal. The path is named after Nishida Kitaro, a Kyoto University professor who was known to meditate on this path during his walks. Kyoto Imperial PalaceThe palace is the former residence of the Japanese emperor, and here visitors can explore beautifully manicured gardens and the grand traditional architecture. Nishiki MarketThe market is also known as Kyoto’s kitchen and is filled with local snacks, fresh seafood, pickles, and handmade crafts. Natural Attractions and Outdoor Spaces Mount KuramaSituated in the north of Kyoto, the mountain is known for its scenic hiking trails and the Kurama Onsen hot spring. It is a brilliant way to escape from the city’s bustle. Kyoto Botanical GardenThe Kyoto Botanical Garden is a lush garden featuring more than 12,000 plant species. It is a great place to visit year-round, but it is even more beautiful in spring and autumn. Offbeat Places of Interest in Kyoto Eikan-do TempleThough slightly less known to first-time visitors, this temple is a true miracle to behold during autumn when its hillside location is ablaze with colour. Kyoto International Manga MuseumIf you are a manga lover, you are not going to want to leave the museum. With thousands of manga volumes and exhibitions, this will indeed be a paradise for you. Uji A small town near Kyoto, Uji is known for its matcha green tea, the Byodo-in Temple, and serene riverside tea houses. Ohara and Takao VillagesThe villages make for a perfect day trip, and are home to quiet temples, forest trails, and give you a glimpse into Kyoto’s countryside charm. Kyoto’s diversity indeed makes it an unforgettable destination for most travellers. Whether you want to explore ancient shrines, enjoy scenic rivers, or discover hidden alleyways, there are several best things to do in Kyoto.
Kyoto.Food to Eat in Kyoto: Kyoto also happens to be a foodie’s paradise. The city’s culinary heritage is as rich and refined as its temples and traditions. Being Japan’s ancient capital, Kyoto’s style of cooking is a balance between subtle flavour, seasonality, and presentation. It does not matter whether you're dining in a multi-course restaurant or sampling street food at a local market, Kyoto food offers a sensory experience you won’t forget. Traditional Kyoto Cuisine Kaiseki Ryori: This is Kyoto’s signature haute cuisine, which is typically served in traditional restaurants. With beautifully plated seasonal dishes such as sashimi, simmered vegetables, the cuisine also features tofu and grilled fish. Yudofu: This is a simple dish made of boiled tofu in broth and is usually served with dipping sauces. Commonly eaten near Zen temples such as Nanzen-ji, the dish reflects the quiet atmosphere around them. Obanzai Ryori: This is a traditional home-style Kyoto cooking that uses seasonal and locally sourced vegetables. Most dishes are miso-based and include pickles, simmered greens, and small bites. Try Obanzai Takashimaya or Manzara-tei to get a taste of what we are talking about. Shojin Ryori: This is a vegetarian Buddhist meal that is served at temple lodgings and features tofu, pickled vegetables, and rice, prepared with balance and mindfulness. Sushi with a Kyoto Twist Since the city is not sea-facing like Japan, Kyoto had to develop certain unique sushi styles. Saba-zushi: These are pressed mackerel sushi that are slightly pickled and often wrapped in kelp. Inari-zushi: This type of sushi features rice-filled fried tofu pouches, and is sweet and great for vegetarians. Sweets & Snacks Matcha Treats: Kyoto is world-famous for its matcha. You can enjoy matcha parfaits, soft serves, lattes, and wagashi, which are traditional sweets at most cafés across the city. Yatsuhashi: This is a local sweet made of rice flour, sugar, and cinnamon, available both baked and raw, and has fillings made with red bean paste. Mochi Skewers & Takoyaki: You can easily find these in Nishiki Market, and they make for great on-the-go snacking during sightseeing. Where to Eat in Kyoto? Pontocho Alley: Enjoy high-end dining experiences as you overlook the river on this narrow lane. Nishiki Market: This is the best place to try authentic Kyoto street food. Gion District: The spot offers both fine dining and traditional tea houses. From refined meals to humble tofu dishes and sweets, Kyoto cuisine will prove to be a wonderful way to explore the city during your Kyoto travel experience.
Kyoto.Kyoto Currency: It is very important to understand the local currency when you are planning your Kyoto trip. This helps make the travel convenient and helps you understand the budget better. Kyoto’s official currency is the Japanese Yen (JPY), so anytime that you shop for souvenirs in Nishiki Market or enjoy a matcha in Gion, you’ll need Yen in hand for the transactions. Denominations The Yen comes in both coins and notes: Coins: ¥1, ¥5, ¥10, ¥50, ¥100, ¥500 Notes: ¥1,000, ¥2,000, ¥5,000, and ¥10,000 Though the ¥2,000 note is rarely used, it does remain legal tender. The use of ¥1,000 and ¥5,000 bills is most common, especially for food, local transport, and small purchases. Currency Exchange & ATMs Exchange Options: You can easily exchange your home currency at airports like the Kansai International Airport, major banks, post offices, and even currency kiosks that are located near Kyoto Station and Gion. Rates may vary, so it is important to compare before exchanging large amounts. ATMs: You can find ATMs across convenience stores like 7-Eleven, Lawson, and FamilyMart. These ATMs usually accept international cards and offer instructions in English. Cards vs Cash: Most credit and debit cards are accepted at hotels, department stores, and restaurants. However, smaller shops, temples, and local eateries will sometimes accept only cash. This is why it is advisable to carry a sufficient amount of Yen at all times. Exchange Rate As of June 2025, the exchange rate is approximately: 1 INR ≈ 1.80 JPY However, it is important to check the current rates before your trip. Once you better understand how the Kyoto currency works, you will be better able to navigate markets, transport, and local experiences during your Kyoto travel.

table_of_content: Best Places to Visit in Kedarnath
Adventurous things to do in Kedarnath

faq.question: What is the Kedarnath yatra cost?
faq.answer: The cost of your Kedarnath tour will depend on various factors such as your chosen mode of travel, the duration of your trip and the other places that you want to visit when you go on your pilgrimage. There is no fixed cost, so you have to take a quotation based on what your logistics are

faq.question: How to reach Kedarnath from Delhi?
faq.answer: Many people reach Kedarnath via Delhi. You can take a train to Delhi or fly in. Then, you can take a connecting train to Rishikesh or you can directly start your journey towards the holy town via road. For this, you can take a bus or you can hire a cab

faq.question: Is it advisable to go to Kedarnath with the kids?
faq.answer: There are many places to visit in Kedarnath, and many families visit the place every day. While children can go there without any restrictions, you should be cautious when taking your kids there. The trek is difficult and the conditions atop the hill are not very favourable. It is cold and the air is thin, making it hard for a lot of people. If you are taking your children along, try to keep the trip quick and carry all the things they need such as winter clothes and snacks

faq.question: Is it possible to do a Kedarnath day trip?
faq.answer: If you are an expert trekker, you can easily climb up to the holy town, offer your prayers and climb back down on the same day. However, for novice pilgrims, the trek is very difficult and it becomes challenging to return on the same day. If however you need to complete the trip in a day, you can opt for a horseback ride. The horse takes you up in about four hours. Then it takes about three hours to get your darshan done and then another three hours to climb down on horseback. To do a day trip, you need to start very early in the morning, preferably by 5 AM

faq.question: Can I reach Kedarnath by helicopter?
faq.answer: You can reach the Kedarnath summit by helicopter. The cost of a round trip is around INR 8000 per person. The journey takes just about 8 minutes and is a great option for those who want to save time when visiting Kedarnath

faq.question: Are there any resorts in Kedarnath?
faq.answer: No, there are no fancy resorts in Kedarnath. There are some very basic tourist lodges in the area and some dormitories but no large hotels or resorts

faq.question: What is the fastest way to reach Kedarnath?
faq.answer: The fastest way to climb up to Kedarnath is by helicopter. You can pre-book the helicopter tickets online.

faq.question: Are there any hospitals in Kedarnath?
faq.answer: There is a modern hospital in Kedarnath and it has all the amenities to address medical emergencies. There are around 12 beds in the hospital and two doctors are permanently stationed in the hospital

faq.question: Is there an ATM in Kedarnath?
faq.answer: No, you will not find any ATM in Kedarnath. You have to reach Gaurikund, where you can find many ATMs and get the cash you need

faq.question: When is the best season to visit Kedarnath?
faq.answer: The summer months are the best months to visit Kedarnath. The temple is quite crowded in the months of April and May as that is the peak tourist season. The place is relatively emptier in October, just before it closes for the winter, but the weather is very harsh at this time and not very favourable for tourism

Kedarnath.Introduction: Planning a spiritual getaway to the divine house of Kedarnath? Thomas Cook has put together some of the best Kedarnath tour packages, making your pilgrimage experience smooth and memorable. Looking for a budget-friendly Kedarnath road trip or a luxurious helicopter tour? There is a package that suits you just right. Let's see the best Kedarnath tour packages that can make your trip special. The various Kedarnath packages are priced differently, based on the level of luxury, duration, mode of travel, etc. You can choose a suitable package, depending on what your requirements are. While some Kedarnath tour packages include air or train tickets, others just include the Kedarnath sightseeing and hotel options. Ideal for those desiring tailored Kedarnath packages, the Ek Dham Yatra by Road 6-day, 5-night expedition brings you to Kedarnath from Haridwar with Guptkashi as your midway point. It's also an economical option for those favouring a sightseeing road tour along with strong spiritual experiences. The tour price begins at ₹36,000 (reduced from ₹40,000) and offers a pay-in-parts facility of ₹2,057 a month via EMI. Why limit yourself to a single Dham when you can see two? Do Dham Yatra – Badrinath and Kedarnath with a 7-night, 8-day package takes you to both Kedarnath and Badrinath, hence a balanced pilgrimage. You'll have comfortable accommodations, guided tours, and spiritual peace. The cost is ₹53,000 after applying a 10% discount, and EMI options begin at ₹3,036 per month. You can book any suitable Kedarnath yatra package and have a wholesome trip to the Holy destination of Kedarnath which is serenely nestled between the mighty mountains of Uttarakhand. Kedarnath.Interesting Facts about Kedarnath: The government of Uttarakhand has worked very hard to promote Kedarnath tourism. As a result, you find many packages and travel options, making it easy for you to travel. However, most pilgrims return from Kedarnath without learning about the most interesting facts about this holy site. If you are curious to know about these facts, here are some pointers for you: Blessed by Lord Shiva himself Ancient temples Moved out in the winter Use of the Kannada language Not destroyed during the 2013 floods Blessed by Lord Shiva himself It is believed that Lord Shiva descended on the spot of the temple after Nar and Narayana prayed to him. Initially, a Shiv Ling appeared from the Earth and later the Lord himself came and promised that he would always be present on the spot. Thus the temple was created there and Lord Shiva is ardently worshipped. Ancient temples It would make a lot of sense for you to take out some time to visit Kedarnath. This is because the temple is archaeologically and historically very significant. It is over 1200 years old and has stood in the same place for centuries. It is said to have been hidden away under snow for 400 years and rediscovered around 1900 AD. Moved out in the winter The entire town of Kedarnath, including the temple, shuts down two days after Diwali and opens again in the month of April. While the temple is closed, the deity is carried to the holy temple in Ukhimath and worshipped there till the main temple uphill opens up again. Use of the Kannada language One of the most interesting and intriguing facts about Kedarnath is that all the mantras and offerings happen in the Kannada language within the temple. This is very unusual because Kannada is a south Indian Language spoken in the state of Karnataka which is hundreds of miles away. This is a tradition that has been going on since time immemorial and is not one that will ever change. Not destroyed during the 2013 floods Everyone is aware of the devastating flash floods that happened in Kedarnath in 2013. While most of the town was washed away, the temple was not damaged in any major way. A huge boulder just rolled down the hills and got lodged behind the temple. The boulder shielded the temple from the flood waters and didn't allow any damage to fall upon the temple. This was no less of a supernatural occurrence and all the devotees believe this was the greatest proof of divine intervention. These are some of the most fascinating facts about Kedarnath that make the place all the more alluring. When you travel to the town and explore the Kedarnath places to visit, speak to the locals and gather more such interesting information and have a great trip.
Kedarnath.Best Time to Visit Kedarnath: As mentioned above, there is a steady inflow of tourists to the holy land of Kedarnath all year long. Even though the temple remains closed in winter, some adventure seekers and mountaineers visit the town of Kedarnath in winter too. However, the best time to visit Kedarnath is undoubtedly in the summer months. Kedarnath is located amid the Himalayas and we all know how cold it gets in winter in these hills. So if you are planning ahead, try to aim for the summer months. Here’s a lowdown on the different weather conditions in Kedarnath: Summer Winter Monsoon Summer The months of summer are bright and pleasant and many people agree that summer is indeed the best time to visit Kedarnath. The temperatures vary between 15 degrees Celsius and 30 degrees Celsius and on most days there is bright sunshine. The snow melts, and the temple gates are opened, welcoming thousands of pilgrims. The weather is ideal for trekking, darshan, and sightseeing. This is also the peak season for Kedarnath packages, so it's best to book in advance. It may be foggy and windy from time to time, simply because of the high altitude, but in general, this is a great time to visit the town of Kedarnath in Uttarakhand. Since it’s peak season, book your Kedarnath tour packages early to secure good accommodation and travel arrangements. Winter Winters are very cold and gloomy in Kedarnath and it is definitely not the best season to visit Kedarnath! The temperature goes down and sometimes even hits the sub-zero digits. It gets very cold and misty and snowfall is not uncommon either. The bitterly cold climes make it very uncomfortable to look around and enjoy the beautiful places in Kedarnath. Winter is not ideal for visiting Kedarnath since the temple is shut because of the heavy snowfall. The temperatures can go down to -18°C, making it hard to travel. Still, budget travellers can visit neighbouring places with Kedarnath packages at half price. Lord Shiva's idol is transported to Ukhimath where people can perform prayers. Monsoon Not the best season to visit Kedarnath, the monsoons are sudden and unpredictable in the town of Kedarnath. While the rains don't come every day and in great intensity, there can be some heavy spells of rain followed by landslides. This makes it very dangerous to visit the place. Also, after the flash floods of 2013, which caused widespread damage, people are very cautious about travelling to this town during the monsoons. Monsoon heightens the possibility of landslides and roadblocks. The temperature is between 4°C to 11°C, and the regular showers may make your trip difficult. But if you are an adventure-seeker and want to enjoy Kedarnath's greenery, you can go for a well-planned Kedarnath tour with safety measures. Opt for well-planned Kedarnath packages with expert guides and emergency backup plans. Now that you know the Kedarnath best time to visit, plan a trip and explore the beautiful places in Kedarnath at a favourable time of the year. How to Reach Kedarnath? If you are planning your Kedarnath yatra at the moment, you need to explore all your options for reaching the town. There is no airport or railway station in Kedarnath. In fact, there aren't even any motorable roads, so you need to know all the routes that connect this temple destination to the other major cities and towns. Here are some ways in which you can reach Kedarnath: By road By rail By air By road Most people prefer to reach Kedarnath by road. You have many options when it comes to road travel. The nearest motorable road is in Gaurikund, which is the base site for Kedarnath. You can travel to Gaurikund by bus or car. You can reach any major city like Delhi, Haridwar, Rishikesh or Dehradun and take a bus from there. If you have a Kedarnath tour package, the bus ride may be included in it. You can also take your own car or rent a cab and enjoy the breathtaking views on the drive up to Gaurikund and see the beautiful places in Kedarnath. By rail Reaching Kedarnath by train is also an option. It is helpful if you are travelling from a faraway destination like Kolkata or Chennai. In that case, you can take a train to Rishikesh, which has the closest railway station to Kedarnath. There are several trains to Rishikesh from almost every major Indian town and city. Disembark at Rishikesh and then travel to Gaurikund by bus or car. You can opt for a Kedarnath package that includes train travel, as this would make it more convenient for you. By air Dehradun is the closest city with an airport, so if you wish to travel by air, you have to take a flight to Dehradun and then go on your further journey by road. There are many flights that come into Dehradun each day. Alternatively, you can also fly to Delhi and take a scenic road trip to Gaurikund. On the way, you can explore all the places to visit near Kedarnath and make the most of your pilgrimage. The last stretch needs to be trekked, as there are no motorable roads. You can also go on horseback or a palki, which is a seat manually carried by people. Choose the best way to travel and when crossing the majestic mountains on your way to the temple, explore the tourist places near Kedarnath. Feast your eyes on the natural wonders and beautiful hills, cliffs, streams, waterfalls, etc. Take breaks on the trek and click some nice photos to make your memories stronger. Best Places to Visit in Kedarnath While the Kedarnath temple is definitely the biggest attraction, there are other places to visit on your Kedarnath trip as well. Here is a list of the best places in Kedarnath that you should not miss out on: Kedarnath Temple Gaurikund Temple Bhairav Temple Rudra Cave Gandhi Sarovar Kedarnath Temple This goes without saying, but the Kedarnath temple is the top-most important place for you to visit on the trip. Built over 1200 years ago, this is one of the oldest temples in India and is considered to be among the holiest pilgrimage destinations for Hindus all across the globe. Another very beautiful places in Kedarnath would be the temple which is carved out of stone and houses the ancient idol of Lord Shiva. Gaurikund Temple Another very famous and significant temple, the Gaurikund Temple is dedicated to Goddess Parvati. It is believed to be the spot where Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati got married and hence is a very pious and holy temple for Hindus. Most of the Kedarnath tour packages include a visit to this temple, so you should be able to see it without any difficulties. Bhairav Temple Situated to the south of the main Kedarnath Temple, the Bhairav Temple is an ancient temple that looks very interesting and is among the holiest places to see in Kedarnath. Don't forget to pay your respect at this temple when you go on your Kedarnath tour. Rudra Cave Just a half-hour trek from Kedarnath, the Rudra Cave is a fascinating structure built by the Nehru Mountaineering Institute. It has a serene medication centre inside and you should visit this unique spot which is fast becoming a predominantly famous places in Kedarnath. Gandhi Sarovar Situated at the foot of the Kedarnath Dham, the Gandhi Sarovar is a lake that has the most crystal clear water you will see! It is a small lake and is considered to be very holy so include it in your Kedarnath holiday package. Vasuki Tal For those who love adventures, Vasuki Tal is a place they cannot miss. This glacial lake, situated at 14,200 feet, is surrounded by snow-covered summits and provides a scintillating view of the Chaukhamba range. The trekking route to Vasuki Tal is an exhilarating experience, making it an apt inclusion in Kedarnath tour packages for people who love trekking. Guptkashi Guptkashi, which is 47 km short of Kedarnath, is renowned for housing the ancient Vishwanath Temple and Ardhnarishwar Temple. It's an ideal location to visit if you wish for a more relaxed, less hectic spiritual experience for your Kedarnath pilgrimage. Triyuginarayan This is said to be the site where Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati were married. The perpetual fire that continues to burn here is said to have been ignited on the day of their marriage. It's a serene, spiritual halting point that needs to be included in your Kedarnath packages. Tungnath Tungnath, the world's highest temple of Shiva, is both spiritual and visually fascinating. Short hiking from Chopta will reach you here in this lovely temple, hence providing good support to your tour of Kedarnath. Chopta If you are a nature lover, then Chopta is pure heaven. With rolling green meadows, a serene ambience, and breathtaking mountain views, it is ideal for having an additional adventure in your Kedarnath tour packages. These are just some of the places in the list of the best places in Kedarnath. The area surrounding the temple is gorgeous and naturally beautiful. You will find many small streams and waterfalls, cliffs and hillocks and other attractions where you can spend many memorable moments with your loved ones. What to Eat? If you are simply taking a day trip to Kedarnath, you can munch on a lot of snacks such as instant noodles, tea and biscuits, potato chips, etc. There are small stalls on the sides of the road where you can make a pit stop to eat. If you stay the night at Kedarnath in an ashram or lodge, you will get simple, but good food. Do not expect a lot of luxury at these places as the accommodation and food are very basic. All the food served in Kedarnath is pure vegetarian as it is a holy place and alcohol is strictly prohibited. Once you reach Gaurikund, you will have more food options and can enjoy a hearty and wholesome meal consisting of the usual Uttarakhandi delicacies such as Rabri, Gathiya and stuffed parathas with desi ghee. Try to book a Kedarnath tour package that includes food. This will save you the hassle of going around looking for food after your tiring trek. What to Shop in Kedarnath? There are several shops around the temple of Kedarnath that target tourists and sell many interesting items such as souvenirs, gift items, postcards, miniature idols, etc. You can also find genuine Rudraksha at this place. However, you have to be very extremely careful when buying Rudraksha as many sellers sell fake versions of it. Visit the shops with an expert or buy it from a certified seller to be very sure of what you are buying. From enjoying a piping hot plate of instant noodles at a roadside shack to shopping in the bylanes leading up to the temple, you will find some of the most unique tourist experiences in Kedarnath. It is a 16-km trek from Gaurikund. The road is quite safe and you will have hundreds of people trekking up along with you. You will also find the local tour guides, horse keepers and palki riders walking up with you. Make the most of this trek and you will never have any regrets. Offer prayers Once you trek and reach the town of Kedarnath, head over to the temple and offer your prayers. You may have to stand in a queue, but the temple management is very good. The lines are well-managed and there is no pushing and shoving, so you can offer your prayers peacefully. Take stunning photographs When you are on a Kedarnath sightseeing tour or you are walking up to the temple, don't forget to click as many pictures as possible. The town offers a naturally beautiful background for all your pictures and you also get to focus on some of the prettiest and most majestic natural wonders, so don't let go of the lovely photo opportunities. Speak to the locals If you take a horseback ride up to the temple, or simply trek up, don't miss the chance to speak to the locals. Shirdi.Best Time to Visit Shirdi: Choosing the opportune time to embark on a Shirdi family tour package is crucial for ensuring a memorable and fulfilling experience. Let's explore the best time to visit Shirdi, taking into account the specific seasons and their respective charms. Spring (February to March) Spring heralds the onset of a vibrant and rejuvenating atmosphere in Shirdi. During this time, the weather is pleasantly mild, with temperatures ranging from 20°C to 30°C, making it ideal for outdoor exploration and Shirdi sightseeing. The blooming flowers and lush greenery add to the scenic beauty of the region, creating a picturesque backdrop for your Shirdi itinerary. Spring is also considered auspicious for spiritual pursuits, as devotees flock to the Sai Baba Temple to seek blessings and pay homage to the revered saint. Monsoon (July to September) Monsoon transforms Shirdi into a verdant paradise, with the refreshing showers breathing new life into the surroundings. The weather remains cool and pleasant, with temperatures ranging from 20°C to 30°C, offering respite from the summer heat. The lush landscapes and glistening raindrops enhance the spiritual aura of Shirdi, making it a picturesque backdrop for your pilgrimage. However, it is essential to check weather forecasts and road conditions before planning your Shirdi trip, as heavy rains may cause inconvenience in transportation. Summer (April to June) While summer in Shirdi brings warmer temperatures, ranging from 25°C to 40°C, it is still a popular time for tourists, especially for those seeking a quieter and more introspective pilgrimage experience. The relatively lesser crowd allows for a more tranquil visit to the famous tourist places in Shirdi, such as the Sai Baba Temple and Dwarkamai Mosque. However, it is advisable to carry adequate sun protection and stay hydrated while exploring the town during this season with your Shirdi family tour packages. Autumn (October to November) Autumn is perhaps the best time to visit Shirdi, as the weather remains delightful, with temperatures ranging from 20°C to 32°C. The post-monsoon freshness adds to the charm of the town, making it an ideal time for outdoor activities and Shirdi sightseeing packages. The festive season also adds to the vibrancy of Shirdi tourism, with special events and celebrations held at the Sai Baba Temple. The best time to visit Shirdi largely depends on personal preferences and interests. Whether you prefer the lush landscapes of monsoon or the mild weather of autumn, Shirdi offers something for every pilgrim throughout the year. Shirdi.Places to Visit in Shirdi: Shirdi, steeped in spirituality and revered by millions, boasts a plethora of enchanting destinations that are a must-visit for pilgrims and tourists alike. Here are some of the most popular and iconic places to see in Shirdi: Sai Baba Temple Undoubtedly the focal point of Shirdi tourism, the Sai Baba Temple is a sacred shrine dedicated to the revered saint, Sai Baba. Devotees from all walks of life flock to this holy abode to seek blessings and pay homage to the beloved saint. The serene ambiance and divine aura of the temple create a tranquil atmosphere, offering solace and spiritual enlightenment to visitors. Chavadi A testament to the saint's simple yet profound teachings, Chavadi is a historical site where Sai Baba spent alternate nights during the last decade of his life. Pilgrims visit this humble dwelling to pay their respects and partake in the rituals held to honour the saint's legacy. The aura of spirituality and reverence that permeates Chavadi makes it a must-visit destination for spiritual seekers and devotees on their Shirdi family tour packages. Dwarkamai Another significant landmark in Shirdi, Dwarkamai holds immense religious significance for devotees of Sai Baba. It was the abode of Sai Baba during his lifetime and served as a shelter for the destitute and a sanctuary for spiritual seekers. Today, it stands as a symbol of compassion and service as one of the best tourist places in Shirdi, attracting pilgrims who come to experience the divine presence of Sai Baba. Shani Shingnapur While not located within Shirdi per se, Shani Shingnapur is a nearby destination that is often included in Shirdi one day tour. This unique village is renowned for its open-air temple dedicated to Lord Shani, the Hindu deity associated with the planet Saturn. Devotees believe that the village is protected by the benevolent gaze of Lord Shani, making it a popular pilgrimage site. Lendi Baug For those seeking moments of tranquillity amidst nature, Lendi Baug offers a serene retreat away from the hustle and bustle of the town making it one of best Shirdi places to visit. This beautiful garden was tended to by Sai Baba himself and is adorned with lush greenery, flowering plants, and serene pathways. Visitors can stroll through the garden, meditate in its peaceful surroundings, and connect with nature in a meaningful way. Shirdi tour plan is replete with sacred landmarks and spiritual treasures that beckon pilgrims and travellers from far and wide with top places to visit in Shirdi. From the iconic Sai Baba Temple to the serene environs of Lendi Baug, each destination offers a glimpse into the divine legacy of Sai Baba and the timeless wisdom he imparted to humanity. Shirdi.What to Eat in Shirdi?: When it comes to culinary delights, Shirdi offers a delectable array of dishes that tantalise the taste buds and leave a lasting impression on every visitor. Here are five must-try delicacies on your journey with Shirdi vacation packages, that showcase the rich flavours and cultural heritage of this sacred town: Sabudana Khichdi A popular breakfast delicacy in Shirdi is Sabudana Khichdi, a savoury and nutritious dish made from soaked tapioca pearls cooked with roasted peanuts, potatoes, cumin seeds, and green chillies. Light yet filling, Sabudana Khichdi is a favourite among those observing religious fasts or looking for a wholesome and satisfying meal. With its delicate balance of flavours and textures, this traditional dish is a must-try for anyone visiting with Shirdi packages. Poha A quintessential breakfast dish beloved by locals and visitors alike, Poha is a flavourful and satisfying option to kickstart your day in Shirdi. Made from flattened rice flakes cooked with onions, tomatoes, green chillies, and a blend of aromatic spices, Poha is a wholesome and comforting meal that pairs perfectly with a cup of piping hot chai. Whether you're exploring the famous tourist places in Shirdi or embarking on a sightseeing Shirdi tour packages, a plate of Poha is sure to fuel your adventures and keep you energised throughout the day. Misal Pav A spicy and flavourful Maharashtrian delicacy, Misal Pav is a culinary delight that should not be missed during your trip with Shirdi packages. Made from sprouted lentils cooked in a spicy gravy, topped with crunchy farsan (crispy snacks), chopped onions, coriander leaves, and a squeeze of lime juice, Misal Pav packs a punch of flavours that will leave you craving for more. Served with soft and fluffy pav (bread rolls), this iconic dish is a favourite among locals and tourists alike. Modak No culinary journey in Shirdi is complete without indulging in the divine sweetness of Modak, a traditional Maharashtrian sweet dumpling offered as prasad (offering) to deities. Made from rice flour dough filled with a sweet mixture of jaggery, grated coconut, and cardamom powder, Modak is steamed to perfection and garnished with ghee-roasted cashews and raisins. With its heavenly taste and auspicious significance, Modak is a must-have delicacy during your visit to famous tourist places in Shirdi. Thalipeeth A traditional Maharashtrian flatbread, Thalipeeth is a nutritious and wholesome delicacy that makes for a hearty meal while travelling with Shirdi packages. Made from a blend of multigrain flour, spices, onions, coriander leaves, and grated vegetables, Thalipeeth is flattened into a round shape and cooked on a griddle till golden brown and crispy. Served hot with a dollop of butter or homemade chutney, Thalipeeth is a delicious and fulfilling dish that showcases the culinary diversity of Shirdi. Shirdi tour plan offers a culinary tapestry rich in flavours, textures, and cultural influences, providing visitors with a gastronomic experience like no other. From savoury breakfast delights like Poha and Sabudana Khichdi to spicy treats like Misal Pav and Thalipeeth, and finally, the divine sweetness of Modak, there's something to please every palate when visiting famous tourist places in Shirdi. Explore the culinary treasures of Shirdi during your trip with our specially curated Shirdi tour packages and embark on a flavourful journey of discovery and delight.

Shopping in Shirdi offers a delightful blend of spirituality, culture, and local flavours. Whether you're purchasing Sai Baba memorabilia, traditional attire, handicrafts, or indulging in street food delights, the markets of Shirdi have something to offer for every visitor. Plan your shopping spree along with your Shirdi tour itinerary and make the most of your visit to this sacred town.
Shirdi.Interesting Facts about the Shirdi place: Discovering Shirdi goes beyond its spiritual significance, as the town is steeped in history and filled with fascinating facts that intrigue visitors from all walks of life. Here are ten interesting facts about Shirdi that every traveller should know while visiting famous tourist places in Shirdi: Sai Baba arrived in Shirdi in the mid-19th century and spent over fifty years in the town, spreading his message of love, compassion, and universal brotherhood. His teachings continue to inspire millions of devotees worldwide. The name "Shirdi" is derived from the word "Sai" and "Ishwari," meaning the abode of Sai Baba. It reflects the town's association with the revered saint and spiritual leader, Sai Baba, who resided here during his lifetime. After his passing in 1918, Sai Baba's mortal remains were interred at the Samadhi Mandir in Shirdi, which has since become a place of pilgrimage and devotion for millions of devotees. Shirdi is a melting pot of diverse cultures, languages, and traditions, welcoming visitors from all corners of the globe with open arms. It exemplifies the spirit of unity in diversity, as people of different backgrounds come together in reverence of Sai Baba. Dwarkamai, the sacred abode of Sai Baba in Shirdi, houses a perpetually burning flame known as the Dhuni. Devotees believe that the Dhuni symbolises Sai Baba's divine presence and blessings. Throughout the year, Shirdi comes alive with vibrant celebrations and festivals honouring Sai Baba's life and teachings. From Guru Purnima to Ram Navami, devotees gather in large numbers to participate in religious rituals, bhajans, and cultural programmes. Shirdi is blessed with abundant natural beauty, with lush gardens and flowering plants adorning its landscapes. The vibrant colours and fragrant blooms add to the town's serene ambiance and spiritual allure. While rooted in tradition and spirituality, Shirdi also offers modern amenities and facilities to cater to the needs of contemporary travellers. From luxury hotels and restaurants to shopping centres and recreational activities, Shirdi ensures a comfortable and memorable experience for visitors. Sai Baba was renowned for his selfless service and charitable activities, providing food, shelter, and solace to the needy and destitute. His teachings inspire philanthropic initiatives and humanitarian efforts in Shirdi to this day. Sai Baba's teachings transcend geographical boundaries, attracting devotees from around the world to Shirdi. Visitors from countries far and wide embark on spiritual journeys to seek Sai Baba's blessings and grace while visiting the famous tourist places in Shirdi.

table_of_content: The historical and cultural importance of Kashi
Traditions and rituals practised in Kashi
Things to Know While Visiting Kashi
Kashi.Introduction: Kashi, also called Varanasi or Banaras, is one of the most sacred places in India for people of the Hindu faith. The place has immense religious, historical and cultural significance and is among the most visited pilgrimage places in the country. If you are planning your Kashi trip, go through the following pointers for information on Kashi tourism and popular Kashi tour packages;
Kashi.Most Popular Kashi Tour Packages: Tour Packages NameDurationPriceAnant Kashi Ayodhya Yatra5 Nights 6 DaysRs. ₹41 400
Kashi.The historical and cultural importance o: Kashi, or Varanasi, is one of the world’s oldest living cities. The city is massively tied to Hindu mythology and is considered the ultimate pilgrimage spot for people of the Hindu faith. According to Hindu legends, this city is believed to be the holy abode of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati. Though the true origin of the city is unknown, historical evidence suggests it has been a major centre for civilisation and education for more than 300 years. Perched on the banks of the sacred Ganges River, Kashi is considered an auspicious place to die. Popular beliefs suggest that those who die in Kashi achieve “Moksha”, which is liberation from the cycle of life and death. In addition, bathing in the holy Ganges here is one of the best things to do in Kashi. It is said to wash away the sins of mortals. Throughout thousands of years, aspects like religion, culture, art, philosophy, knowledge, crafts, etc., have flourished in Kashi. Apart from Hindus, Kashi is also a pilgrimage spot for Jains, as it is said to be the birthplace of the twenty-third Tirthankar, Parshvanath. Sarnath, the place where Lord Buddha gave his first sermon, making it one of the holiest places for Buddhists, is located in the district of Varanasi, just 10 km from the holy city of Kashi. If we look in the pages of history, we will find that the city was part of one of the most powerful kingdoms in ancient India. The Kingdom of Kashi was one of the sixteen Mahajanapadas in Northern India, back in the 6th century BCE. Make your dream trip to Varanasi a reality with the Kashi tour packages by Thomas Cook.
Kashi.Traditions and rituals practised in Kash: The people of Kashi celebrate their traditional holidays in a grand way. Festivals like Makar Sankranti, Dev Diwali, Shravan Maah, Maha Shivarati, Holi, Akshaya Tritiya, etc., are a few examples of major festivals in this holy city. As for rituals and customs, taking a holy dip in the Ganges River while uttering mantras repetitively is a major one. For your tour to Kashi, you can choose a time that covers one of these major festivals. Other Hindu rituals that are practised in Kashi include Samskara, Puja, Vrata, Yagnya or Homa, etc. The majority of rituals in Kashi are performed with the intent to fulfil one’s dharma, to repay specific obligations or debts (Rin). Check out Thomas Cook for ideal deals on Kashi travel packages.
Kashi.Places to Visit in Kashi: In addition to visiting the renowned temples in Kashi, make sure to also check out these popular attractions; Other religious places to visit in Kashi Dashashwamedh Ghat There are more than 80 ghats in Kashi and among them, the Dashashwamedh Ghat is the most popular and common inclusion in many Kashi tour packages. It is the main ghat of the holy city, perched on the side of the sacred Ganges River, only a 10-minute walk from the Kashi Vishwanath Temple. A legend suggests that Lord Brahma created this ghat to welcome Lord Shiva. The renowned Ganga Aarti and tons of other spiritual rituals take place at this ghat, making it one of the best places to see in Kashi. Alamgir Mosque Alamgir Mosque, also known as Aurangzeb’s Mosque, is one of the most prominent Kashi tourist attractions. The mosque is also of immense historical importance in the region and is a great example of India’s classical architecture, showing a blend of Hindu and Islamic architecture. Make sure to check it out on your tour to Kashi. Gyan Vapi Well The Gyan Vapi Well is among the most renowned Kashi tourist places for spiritual visits. It is part of the Kashi Vishwanath Temple and is located within the temple premises. The water of this holy well is considered sacred and devotees even drink it. It is one of the most interesting places to see in Kashi. Pick from a wide range of budget-friendly Kashi travel packages on our website. Unique features of Kashi The beautiful city of Varanasi is nestled on the western bank of the river Ganga. It is one of the most sacred rivers in India according to Hinduism and is believed to wash away impurities and sins from the souls of mortals. Besides its religious significance, the river adds a scenic charm to the entire cityscape. It is one of the most iconic Kashi places to visit.
Kashi.Temples to Visit in Kashi: Among the thousands of temples in Kashi, here are some of the most popular ones that you should visit; Shri Kashi Vishwanath Temple The Shri Kashi Vishwanath Temple is among the most important and sacred Hindu shrines in India. It is one of the 12 Jyotirlingas dedicated and has stood for thousands of years. There are many legends associated with this shrine and its mention can be found in the ancient scriptures like Puranas. The temple is also called Golden Temple and its presiding deity is Lord Vishwanath, which is an incarnation of Lord Shiva. The temple complex is home to many smaller shrines, some of which are dedicated to other deities, and a holy well, called Gyaan Vapi. The main Shivalinga at this temple is 24 inches tall and 35 inches in circumference. All popular Kashi tour packages feature this temple on their Kashi itinerary. Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple Next on the list of popular temples to visit in Kashi is the Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple. The shrine is dedicated to Lord Hanuman, who stands as an epitome of courage, strength and devotion. The shrine was founded by a famous poet, saint and Hindu preacher, Sri Goswami Tulsidas in the early 16th century. The term “Sankat Mochan” means the “remover of sorrows”. So, make sure to add it to the itinerary of your Kashi tour. The temple is often included in popular Kashi travel packages. Durga Temple The Durga Mandir is one of the major Kashi places to visit for spiritual tourism. The temple stands out for its beautiful and intricate architecture and vibrant red colour. The shrine is dedicated to Goddess Gurga, who is considered the mother of the universe and the destroyer of evil. Inside the shrine, there are statues of the goddess in different forms, including Durga Ashtabhuja, Mahishasurmardini, etc. You can find Kashi trip packages featuring this site on our website. Adjacent to the temple, there is a sacred pond where devotees take a holy dip to purify themselves before entering the shrine for darshan. The shrine was constructed back in the 18th century by Rani Bhabani of Natore and is a major pitstop for devotees on their Kashi tour. Bharat Mata Mandir The Bharat Mata Mandir is a very unique shrine and should be a part of the Kashi trip plan of every patriot. This temple is actually dedicated to our motherland, Mother India, also known as Bharat Mata. Inside the shrine stands a marble statue of Bharat Mata, portrayed with four arms symbolising love, peace, power, and knowledge. The temple also has a museum, housing components related to India’s history and culture. It is a must-visit attraction in Kashi for Indians and a popular inclusion in many Kashi trip packages. Temple Name Opening Time & Closing Time Distance from Varanasi Junction Railway Station Shri Kashi Vishwanath Temple 4 AM to 11 AM & 12 PM to 7 PM 4.4 Km Sankat Mochan Hanuman Temple 5 AM to 10 PM 6.4 Km Durga Temple 4 AM to 1 PM & 3 PM to 8 PM 5.6 Km Bharat Mata Mandir 8 AM to 5:30 PM 1.5 km
Kashi.Things to Know While Visiting Kashi: Here are some noteworthy pointers that you should take care of on your Kashi trip; When visiting the Kashi Vishwanath Temple or any other religious shrine in Kashi, make sure to dress modestly and remove shoes outside before entering the temple premises. There may be security checks at the temple entrance, so be sure to cooperate with the personnel and follow their instructions. Photography is strictly prohibited inside many temples in Kashi, so make sure to adhere to this rule. When inside the temple, maintain a respectful demeanour and avoid being loud or causing any kind of disturbance to others. Non-Hindus are not allowed to enter Shri Kashi Vishwanath Temple. If you want to learn more about the place on your visit, consider hiring a local Kashi tour guide.
Kashi.Best Time to Visit Kashi: Make sure to research the best time for the trip before looking for Kashi tour packages. Here’s a season-wise Kashi travel guide; Winter in Kashi Winter brings a soothing charm to the region, featuring an average temperature of 5-15°C. It covers the months of November to February. The cool weather during this season makes it the best time to visit Kashi for temple tours, Kashi sightseeing and other outdoor activities. Sipping hot tea by the riverside during mist-covered early winter mornings is a fascinating experience. Summer in Kashi Summer extends from March to June in Kashi. The weather stays on the warm and dry side, with scorching heat throughout the day. The average temperature hovers between the low and high 30s, with occasional peaks as high as 45°C. Due to the extreme heat, it is not an ideal time to visit Kashi. If you are travelling around these months, make sure to stick to early morning or late evening outings. Get Kashi trip packages for any season on Thomas Cook. Monsoon in Kashi Monsoon in Kashi extends from July to October and brings a sigh of relief after the scorching summer heat. The season is marked by heavy rainfall frequently with an average temperature range of 25-30°C. If you don’t mind the rain, it can be a fascinating time to visit Kashi. Exploring the rain-soaked city is an experience of its own calibre.
Kashi.Quotes and Inspirations: Kashi, or Varanasi, is one of the holiest places for Hindus in India. Dotted with thousands of temples, the divine aura and serene ambience are evident through every nook and corner of this sacred place. The smooth-flowing Ganga River, devotees participating in holy rituals at the ghats and people standing in queues for darshan of the gods and goddesses, are all part of the city’s soul. All while planning the trip, we had one quote by Ramana Maharshi resonating in our minds, where he said, “The body is the temple; the Jiva is God (Siva). If one worships him with the ‘I am He’ thought, one will gain release”. It didn’t take much time for us to get accustomed to this soothing and calming vibe of the city during our visit. From the station to our hotel, going through narrow paths and catching glimpses of several temples along the way, our excitement increased by tenfold. To get the full picture of Kashi, we spent hours chatting with the locals and visitors to learn about their experiences, ate the Kashi famous food, went on general Kashi sightseeing and participated in local customs. Our visit to the temple started off with the revered Shri Kashi Vishwanath Mandir and it was an experience that we will never forget. On our visit, we felt like we were getting enshrouded by history, culture and divinity. The sight of the lord felt comforting and seeking holy blessings was as if we were children visiting our parents with our problems and waiting for a solution to get handed to us. If you want the best Varanasi experience, consider the Kashi travel packages by Thomas Cook.
Kashi.What to Eat in Kashi?: With the delicious range of Kashi famous food items, you will never go hungry. Here’s what you can consider ordering; Choora Matar During winter, the street food stalls across Varanasi offer tons of delightful dishes, and one such popular food is Choora Matar. It is made from flattened rice soaked in desi ghee and cooked with green peas, milk, saffron, raisins and a few basic spices. It is the perfect local Kashi food for early morning breakfast in Kashi. Tamatar Chaat This spicy and tangy delight is one of the most popular street foods in Kashi. It is a type of aloo chat that is made with boiled potatoes, green chillies, tomatoes, onions and coriander leaves. Garam masala, ginger and red chilli powder are also added to the mix to enhance its taste. Popular Kashi packages come with meals as a standard inclusion. Kachori Sabzi Kachori Sabzi is the most famous dish to try in Kashi on your trip. This dish is a combination of crispy kachori, which are stuffed with spices and lentils, and a flavourful sabzi, which is made using boiled potatoes, chillies, and a handful of spices. Together, they make a delectable, must-try combo. Chena Dahi Vada After trying the spicy Kashi food items, if you wish for something savoury and sweet, try Chena Dahi Vada. It is a dish prepared using mashed potatoes, cottage cheese, cornflour and grated ginger, which is shaped in small vadas and deep fried in oil. The Chena Vadas are then topped with sweet curd, with a mix of tangy chutney, tamarind and masalas.
Kashi.Shopping in Kashi: If you wish to go shopping on your Kashi tour, here are some things you can consider buying; The holy water of Ganga One of the most popular things devotees and tourists buy on their Kashi trip is Ganga Jal. When visiting the ghats, you can find many vendors selling plastic bottles and copper cans. If you purchase such, the shopkeeper will fill out the bottle with the holy water from the ghat themselves and hand it to you. Get attractive deals on Kashi packages on our website. Banarasi silk sarees Banarasi Silk Sarees are massively popular across the country. It is one such piece of apparel that Indian women like to have in their collection, even if they don’t wear it often. This beautiful type of saree is available in all kinds of vibrant colours and with eye-catching designs. Add Thateri Bazaar to your Kashi travel plan for Banarasi silk saree shopping. Rudraksha Mala Another popular item that tourists like to buy on their Kashi visit is Rudraksha Mala. Made from the polished seed of the Rudraksha tree, it is a kind of organic jewellery that Hindu devotees wear. It is believed that this mala protects the wearer against evil spirits and bad energies. Find Kashi trip packages with interesting itineraries on our website. Stone and wood-carved items Kashi is a hub of art and culture and the local handicraft is a great example of it. You can find all kinds of intricate, stone and wood-carved items in the Kashi markets that highlight the skilled craftsmanship of local artisans. From stone-carved idols of deities to wooden toys and souvenirs, there’s a lot to choose from.
